title: Hypserpa funifera
taxonomy: Hypserpa funifera is a species of flowering plant in the family Convolvulaceae, commonly known as the morning glory family. This plant belongs to the genus Hypserpa, which is a group of flowering plants native to tropical regions. The Convolvulaceae family is a large and diverse group, comprising over 1,800 species of plants, many of which are vines or climbing plants. Hypserpa funifera is a climbing plant with heart-shaped leaves and small, blue-purple flowers. The plant is native to the tropical regions of Asia and Australia.

intro: Hypserpa funifera, also known as the "Kadupul flower" or "Queen of the night", is a flowering plant native to Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia, characterized by its unique ability to bloom only at night and wilt by dawn. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which are believed to aid in the treatment of various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Hypserpa funifera include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional Ayurvedic and Unani medicine, the plant is often used to prepare various herbal remedies, including decoctions, infusions, and ointments, which are used to treat a range of health issues.
